name: Stanford Prison Experiment
description: The Stanford Prison Experiment (SPE) was a 1971 social psychology study conducted by Philip Zimbardo at Stanford University. It aimed to investigate the psychological effects of perceived power, focusing on the struggle between prisoners and prison officers. Volunteer students were randomly assigned to roles as either guards or prisoners in a mock prison environment. The experiment, initially planned for two weeks, was halted after only six days due to the rapidly escalating psychological distress and anti-social behaviors exhibited by the participants. Proponents argue the SPE demonstrates the powerful influence of situational factors on human behavior, suggesting that ordinary people can readily adopt roles and behaviors associated with authority or subservience, leading to dehumanization and abuse. Some interpretations extend this to explain real-world instances of prison abuse and even broader societal power dynamics. However, the SPE has faced significant criticism. Methodological flaws include Zimbardo's dual role as principal investigator and 'prison superintendent,' which compromised objectivity and potentially influenced participant behavior. Critics also point to selection bias, as volunteers may have been predisposed to authoritarian or submissive tendencies. Furthermore, the ecological validity of the experiment has been questioned, arguing that the artificial environment doesn't accurately reflect real prison conditions. More recent analyses suggest participants were actively playing roles they believed Zimbardo expected of them, rather than simply succumbing to situational pressures. The ethical implications of the SPE are also widely debated, given the psychological harm inflicted on participants.
subtitle: Power, Conformity, and Situational Influence

claims.claim: Situational forces can override individual disposition.
claims.evidence: Rapid changes in participant behavior, with guards becoming abusive and prisoners exhibiting signs of distress.
claims.criticism: Selection bias: volunteers may have been predisposed to certain behaviors. Demand characteristics: participants may have acted according to perceived expectations.

claims.evidence: Guards readily adopted authoritarian and abusive behaviors.
claims.criticism: Not all guards behaved abusively. Some attempted to be 'good' guards. The definition of 'corruption' is subjective in this context.

claims.claim: Dehumanization is a consequence of power imbalance.
claims.evidence: Prisoners were subjected to degrading treatment and lost their sense of identity.
claims.criticism: The extent of dehumanization is debated. Participants were aware of the experimental setting, mitigating real-world consequences.

keyFigures.name: Carlo Prescott
keyFigures.role: Former prisoner and consultant; later critiqued the experiment

keyFigures.name: Christina Maslach
keyFigures.role: Social psychologist who ended the experiment due to ethical concerns

mythologyConnections: Themes of power dynamics and societal roles found in various mythologies.
The concept of 'the shadow self' - the dark side of human nature revealed under specific conditions.

timeline.event: Initial publication of results and widespread media attention
Increasing scrutiny and criticism of the experiment's methodology and ethical implications
